name: Elections, Judiciary in India, and Rule of Law

sourceRefs.label: 2026_q page 36 Q35 (UK=Bicameral Legislature, US=removal procedure for SC/HC Judges — correctly paired; USSR/Australia mismatched, with answer key)
2026_q page 30 Q29 (Article 368 amendments touching Judiciary/State powers need State ratification, with answer key)
2024_q page 43 Q61 (Election Commission IS constitutional; NDC and NITI Aayog are NOT, with answer key)
2017_q1 page 20 Q137 (Article 136 — Special Leave Petition power of the Supreme Court, stashed earlier, with answer key)
drishtijudiciary.com - Article 324 Election Commission details; vajiramandravi.com - Article 124 SC appointment/retirement

researchNotes: 4 real hits plus 1 stashed real hit, reused here. Real exam evidence often tests Judiciary/Elections by ELIMINATION against a similar-sounding wrong body or country — Election Commission (Art 324, constitutional) vs. NDC/NITI Aayog (extra-constitutional); UK/US's genuine borrowed features vs. USSR/Australia's wrongly-attached ones. This topic deliberately doesn't re-explain Judicial Review or the S.R. Bommai case in depth since those are already covered in a4-salient-features-of-constitution — cross-reference there for those facts.

reading.intro: Covers the Election Commission (Article 324), the Supreme Court/High Court judiciary (Articles 124-147, 214-231), and the foundational concept of Rule of Law. Real exam evidence favours testing these via comparison-and-elimination against similar bodies or borrowed-from-country mix-ups.

qa.question: What is the 'Rule of Law', per A.V. Dicey's three classical principles?
qa.answer: (1) Supremacy of law / absence of arbitrary power — no one can be punished except for a clear breach of law established through ordinary legal process; (2) Equality before the law — everyone, regardless of rank or status, is subject to the same law and ordinary courts; (3) Predominance of the legal spirit — individual rights are best secured not by abstract constitutional declarations alone, but by their actual enforcement and protection through court decisions.

qa.question: Real exam: Which features are correctly matched to the country India borrowed them from — UK/Bicameral Legislature, USSR/Independent Judiciary, Australia/Five Year Plans, US/Removal procedure for SC and HC Judges?
qa.answer: UK → Bicameral Legislature is CORRECT. US → the procedure for removal of Supreme Court and High Court Judges is also CORRECT. The other two are WRONG: Independent Judiciary is actually borrowed from the USA (not USSR), and Five Year Plans are borrowed from the USSR/Soviet Union (not Australia) — confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: Is the Election Commission a constitutional body? What about the National Development Council and NITI Aayog?
qa.answer: The Election Commission IS a constitutional body (established under Article 324). The National Development Council (NDC) and NITI Aayog are BOTH extra-constitutional/non-constitutional bodies — created by executive/Cabinet resolutions, not by the Constitution itself — confirmed by a real exam question testing exactly this distinction (alongside the Finance Commission, which like the EC, IS constitutional, under Article 280).

qa.question: How is the Chief Election Commissioner protected to ensure independence, and how can other Election Commissioners be removed?
qa.answer: The CEC can be removed only in the same manner and on the same grounds as a Supreme Court judge (i.e., via the impeachment-style process), and his/her service conditions cannot be changed to their disadvantage after appointment — both designed to insulate the CEC from executive pressure. Other Election Commissioners can be removed on the CEC's recommendation.

qa.question: What are the qualifications and retirement age for a Supreme Court judge, per Article 124?
qa.answer: Must have been a High Court judge for at least 5 years, OR an advocate in a High Court for at least 10 years, OR (in the President's opinion) be a distinguished jurist. Appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Supreme Court Collegium. Retirement age: 65 years (compare: High Court judges retire at 62 years).

qa.question: Real exam: Under Article 136, what special power does the Supreme Court have?
qa.answer: The power to grant 'Special Leave to Appeal' (SLP) from any judgment, decree, determination, or order passed by any court or tribunal in India (except military tribunals/courts-martial) — confirmed by a real exam question.

qa.question: Real exam: Which categories of Constitutional Amendment (under Article 368) require ratification by not less than half of the State Legislatures, in addition to Parliament's special majority?
qa.answer: Amendments that touch the powers/provisions of the JUDICIARY (Supreme Court and High Courts) and amendments that CHANGE THE POWERS OF THE STATES (federal distribution of powers) — both require State ratification, confirmed by a real exam question. By contrast, amendments merely adding a new principle, or even altering Fundamental Rights, do NOT require State ratification — only Parliament's special majority.

questions.prompt: A.V. Dicey's 'Rule of Law' includes which of the following principles?
options: Supremacy of the Executive over courts
Equality before the law for all persons
Special legal immunity for government officials
Laws can be applied retroactively without restriction

questions.prompt: Which of the following feature-to-country pairings is CORRECT?
The procedure for removal of Supreme Court and High Court Judges was borrowed from:
Which of the following is a CONSTITUTIONAL body (i.e., established directly by the Constitution)?
questions.explanation: Election Commission (Article 324) — NDC and NITI Aayog are both extra-constitutional.

questions.prompt: The Chief Election Commissioner can be removed from office only:
options: In the same manner and on the same grounds as a Supreme Court judge
questions.explanation: Same manner/grounds as a Supreme Court judge.

questions.prompt: A Supreme Court judge retires at the age of, while a High Court judge retires at:
questions.explanation: SC judges retire at 65; HC judges at 62.

questions.prompt: Under Article 136, the Supreme Court has the power to:
options: Grant Special Leave to Appeal from any court or tribunal in India (except military tribunals)

questions.prompt: An amendment to the Constitution that changes the powers of the States requires ratification by:
options: A simple majority of Parliament only
Not less than half of the State Legislatures, in addition to Parliament's special majority
questions.explanation: Not less than half the State Legislatures, plus Parliament's special majority.

questions.prompt: Does an amendment merely abolishing or altering Fundamental Rights require ratification by State Legislatures?
options: No — only Parliament's special majority is needed
Only if more than 5 rights are affected
